title: Scotch egg
subtitle: Soft-boiled egg in sausagemeat, breadcrumbed and deep-fried
excerpt: A soft-boiled egg encased in seasoned sausagemeat, coated in breadcrumbs and deep-fried until the casing is golden and crisp. Cutting through the crisp shell to a still-yielding yolk is the goal. A picnic and pub classic that is technically straightforward once the egg timing is correct.
sourceNotes: The Scotch egg has no established Scottish origin. It appears in Fortnum and Mason's records from 1738 as a portable travelling food. The modern form with breadcrumbing and deep-frying became standard in the 20th century. The soft-boiled centre is a contemporary preference, the Victorian Scotch egg used a hard-boiled egg.

content.text: The egg timing is the most critical variable. For a soft centre with no green ring: bring the water to the boil, lower the eggs in, cook for exactly 6 minutes, then transfer to ice water for 2 minutes. The egg will continue cooking slightly inside the sausagemeat during frying, a 6-minute boil produces a just-set white and a flowing yolk after frying.
The sausagemeat casing must be the same thickness all around the egg, thin patches will burst during frying. Wet hands make the meat easier to handle without sticking.

Bring a pan of water to the boil. Lower in 4 of the {{eggs}} and cook for exactly 6 minutes. Transfer to ice water immediately. Peel carefully once cool. Mix the {{sausagemeat}} with {{english-mustard}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Divide into 4 equal portions. Flatten each into a thin oval in your wet hands. Dust each peeled egg lightly in {{plain-flour}}, place in the centre of a sausagemeat portion, and wrap the meat around the egg, pressing firmly to seal with no gaps. Roll each in flour, then dip in the 2 beaten eggs, then roll in {{breadcrumbs-dried}}, pressing to coat well. Chill for 30 minutes. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} to 170°C. Deep-fry in batches for 7 to 8 minutes, turning halfway, until deep golden brown. Drain on kitchen paper. Cut in half to serve.

items.fix: Exactly 6 minutes from boiling water, then immediate ice bath. Even an extra minute produces a firmer yolk
items.cause: Egg cooked too long in the initial boil
items.symptom: Yolk hard rather than flowing after frying

items.fix: Ensure the sausagemeat is a consistent 1 cm thick all around. Fry at 170°C, not higher, too hot crisps the outside before the meat inside is cooked
items.cause: Thin patches, or frying temperature too high

items.fix: The flour-egg-breadcrumb sequence must be thorough. Press the breadcrumbs on firmly. Chilling the assembled Scotch eggs for 30 minutes sets the coating
items.cause: Coating not adhering, or oil too hot

content.text: The Scotch egg is a picnic and pub food, eaten at room temperature or slightly warm. It is one of the few portable British dishes that requires serious technique. The soft-boiled centre has elevated it from a convenience snack to something worth making properly.
